title: Pakistani Mutton Karahi

ingredients.name: ginger, julienned (half for cooking, half for garnish)

steps.text: Heat oil and ghee together in a large karahi over high heat. Add the crushed garlic and half the ginger and fry for 1 minute until golden. Add the mutton pieces and sear on all sides until the meat is browned and sealed, about 8–10 minutes.

Add the chopped tomatoes, red chilli powder, turmeric, coriander powder, and salt. Stir well, reduce heat to medium, cover and cook for 20 minutes. The tomatoes will collapse into a thick paste — stir every 5 minutes to prevent sticking.

Uncover and increase flame to high. Stir-fry the masala vigorously for 5 minutes, pressing it against the sides, until the oil separates clearly on top and the tomato moisture has cooked off completely.

Beat the curd smooth and add it one spoon at a time, stirring after each addition. Cook on medium for 5 minutes until absorbed, then pour in 200 ml hot water, cover, and simmer on low heat for 35 minutes until the mutton is fully tender and falling from the bone.
Remove the lid and cook on high heat for 5 minutes to tighten the gravy. Add green chillies, coarsely crushed black pepper, and garam masala. Stir and cook for 2 more minutes.
Taste for salt, garnish with remaining ginger julienne and fresh coriander, and serve directly from the karahi with naan or a stack of hot chapati.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: mutton → 750 g chicken on bone (reduce simmering time to 20 minutes); curd → 4 tbsp coconut cream (use if avoiding dairy); ghee → extra cooking oil (ghee adds characteristic richness)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Mutton karahi needs long cooking — use a pressure cooker shortcut: after the initial sear and masala, pressure cook with 150 ml water for 5–6 whistles on medium heat, then open and finish on high heat in the karahi to dry the masala. Kashmiri lal mirch powder from any Indian spice shop gives the authentic reddish colour. Indian goat (bakra) is identical to Pakistani mutton; lamb from a supermarket also works well.
